I installed this screen protector on my Pixel 2. The glass is a little smaller than the phone so it doesn't reach all the way to the edges. That is great if you are using a case. The last screen protector that I tried did reach all the way to the edges and didn't fit in my case. This one is working fine. I don't notice any loss of sensitivity compared to no screen protector. The $7 price is very reasonable. 5 stars.

This power pack is a pricey and bulky for only 6000mah. I have a much cheaper 10,000mah power pack that is about the same size. Also a much cheaper 5000mah power pack that is less than half the size. Both of those require a separate USB power supply to recharge them, but you already have at least one USB power supply to charge your phone (and maybe dozens of them), right?

If you do want an all-in-one power pack that plugs directly into a wall socket and also has a built-in USB-C charging cable, then this may be the product for you. It charges my Google Pixel 2 phone in a couple of hours. It does not support USB Power Delivery fast charging, which would be a great feature to have if you're recharging on the go. Build quality seems pretty good, though the USB-C cable is kind of thin and I worry about long-term durability.

There are a lot of touch-screen styluses on the market these days. Most have rubber tips, while this one has a fiber mesh tip. The fiber tip works well, but I can't say that it is any smoother or more accurate than my much cheaper rubber-tipped stylus. Maybe the fiber tip will last longer? I haven't had any trouble with the cheap rubber-tip stylus yet.

I am happy to see USB-C battery chargers becoming more available at reasonable prices. I have a Google Pixel 2 phone and this battery pack works well for me. It activates my phone's Power Delivery fast charging, indicated by both the phone's status and a light on the battery pack. I like that the battery pack can be charged by USB-C. I do not like that they did not include a USB-C cable, only a useless micro-USB cable. I do like that the battery pack charges at a reasonably fast 2A rate (so recharging doesn't take all night like with old fashioned battery packs).

I do not know why the product title says the capacity is 10000mAh, but the specification chart says the rated capacity is 6250mAh. Unlike many name-brand battery packs, this product is not UL Listed for independent electrical safety testing. For safety reasons, many US corporations will not buy electrical products that are not UL Listed.

Also, the plastic shell of this battery pack feels a little lightweight and cheap compared to other (usually more expensive) products.

I hesitated to order this charger after reading several news reports about the electrical safety of AmazonBasics products. I am very happy to see that this charger is UL Listed, which should indicate that it has been tested for safety by an independent laboratory. For safety reasons, many US companies will only buy electrical devices that are UL Listed or UL Certified.

GaN technology chargers like this are very compact and lightweight, making it great for travel or just carrying with you to the coffee shop. 30W is plenty for my phone and tablet, though my USB-C laptop is happier with 45W or 60W chargers. The charger works great, heating up only slightly when in use.

There is no USB-C cable in the box with the charger. For safety reasons, you should use only a high quality cable with chargers rated 15W or higher. Most companies are including a cable with their chargers to make sure customers use a safe combination.

I only buy UL Certified chargers for my phones and laptops. We've all heard the horror stories about exploding batteries and I can minimize the risk by using chargers that are independently certified for electrical safety. The product description for this charger explicitly says "This 65W usb c charging station is also FCC certified and UL Listed." That statement is false. There is no UL certification printed on the charger or the box or any of the literature. An FCC certification only applies to radio interference, not electrical safety. There is a TÜV SÜD certification, which is apparently means something in the UK, but I have no idea how that corresponds to USA safety standards. Since they advertise USA UL certification and do not present any evidence that is true, I can only give this product 1 star.

I wanted to like this charger because it has two 20W USB-C ports so both the driver and passenger can charge their phones at the same time. Both of our phones use USB-C Power Delivery fast charging, which is an international standard. Unfortunately, this charger does not work with my Google Pixel 6 phone. My phone does work properly with UL Certified USB Power Delivery chargers that I own. This charger is not certified. My phone is somewhat finicky about working with chargers that do not strictly satisfy the charging standards.

These cables have relatively thin plastic housings, which aren't as pretty as the heavy braided housing on some cables. The connectors are also plastic, with good stress relieving. Performance is great so far; these cables are just as fast (for charging) as the fancier looking cables. These cables do not have E-Markers, so they are not good for charging high-power devices like laptops, but they do work fine for smartphones and tablets.

1 foot length is great for use with handheld power packs. A longer cable just adds bulk.

Data speeds are mediocre; if you want fast data transfer, look for cables that advertise USB3 or better, not cables like this that are advertised as charging cables.

Price of $2 each is reasonable for short charging cables.

I really like the belt-clip holster aspect too. I am constantly standing up and sitting down all day, so having to move my phone in and out of my pocket every time gets really old really quick. With the belt clip, I can keep it on my belt all day and never have to worry about leaving it behind because I forgot to put it in my pocket when I got up and walked to another part of the building.

Excellent screen protector, feels really nice to the touch and goes on easily. Here is the problem and it has nothing to do with the screen protector. If you are getting bubbles around the corners or anywhere else that does not go away and it's not dirt or installers error it can be your actual phone display is warped or wavy. I went through 15 not kidding, 15 screen protectors and had bubbles at the same three corners. You may say I suck putting screen protectors on but it's not my first rodeo, I have done many, many, many did I mention MANY PROTECTORS in the past, with 95% success rate. When I finally gave up and left my screen bare, I just so happen to place my phone face up in my office and noticed the reflection of the fluorescent light bend on the screen, as if the screen was not flat. The more I looked the more I saw these waves exactly on the spots of my bubbles. I called my carrier and took the phone to the store to be check and would you believe that they saw the same thing and it was considered a manufacturer defect and my phone was replaced. So to make a long story short, check you phones screen carefully. It may just be you phone and not the screen protectors.

For me, the standout feature of this mount is its adjustability. The mount arm extends, rotates 320-degrees, and has pivots at two places. This means you can adjust to get that “perfect” positioning. So far, it has pretty much stayed in the position I placed it in.

The suction cup adheres well to non-porous surfaces such as glass and smooth plastic. I use it either on the smooth plastic of my dash, on the windshield, or mounted on a weighted base with a smooth plastic insert.

The jaws will accommodate phones up to 3.4 inches. There are other mounts that go to 3.75 inches. Measure your phone before purchasing to avoid disappointment.

Construction is all plastic.

In overall terms, this feels as sturdy as any other mount in its class. As with any mount with expanding jaws, I worry about durability, but so far, so good.

One concept pushed by the manuf6acturer that I like and will try is the idea of using it on counters, tables and desks. On non-porous surfaces, I think the suction cup will work well. It’s certainly worth a try. If it works, I might consider this as something to take along on a trip.

I did not try the wireless charging feature.

On the whole, for the money, I think this is a good deal.

This is really a clever gadget.

You can use its built-in clamp to securely attach it to table tops, seat backs, shelves, anything up to 1.5-inches thick.

Or you can use it as a stand. The device will accommodate smartphones up to 3.75-inches wide and the head will rotate to accommodate landscape and portrait orientation. The head is also rotatable front to back.

You can also use it as pretty well proportioned handle, especially well suited to taking photos and videos.

I can see so many uses for this from the advertised use of clipping it to an airplane tray table (and user suggestions of clipping it to a car sun visor) to a handy way of keeping your phone in front of you if you’re having a cup of coffee or eating or whatever.

The only shortcoming I’ve noted so far is the lack of grippy surfaces: the unit slides – not tips, but slides – on smooth surfaces. If this makes it into my travel kit, I already carry a couple of those jar opener pads. I’d just put one underneath this.

This Perilogics Phone Holder qualifies as one of those gadgets you never thought you needed until the first time you use it.

It’s really cool and very handy.

All was fine until the cosmic coincidence, I installed a tempered glass screen protector, and that is when the stars started to collide with each other.
The glass protector that I installed on the phone is a few thousand of an inch smaller than the actual glass on the phone, and that makes sense, so one can have a little room for positioning the screen protector.
Then I placed this plastic cover, and the lip ends of the cover rest exactly in the empty space, the space not covered by the screen protector.
The result is the cover is always pushing the glass, and it lift it slightly and enough to create a bubble under the screen protector.
What are the chances that the measurements coincide, to give me this problem?
So I cannot say anything bad about the cover, the screen protector or the phone. It is just an unexpected cosmic coincidence.

I do recommend the cover, but if you are going to install a glass screen protector, this cover is not the right one.
So I am searching for a new one, that will rest over the screen protector.

My phone has a massive back camera bump and I was considering getting a screen protector for it. My previous case was thick enough to make the whole back of the phone flat but it came at a cost of the phone being way too big for my pocket so I decided to search for something slimmer that would still protect my device. So I found this and ordered it. I really like it, it's slim but offers decent protection. The S-Pen slot is well protected (it was way too exposed on my last case for some reason), the charging port hole is wide enough to accommodate most USB-C cables and even some USB-C flash drives.

The cover slides over the camera and snaps shut so it doesn't move by accident. You can also snap it open to prevent it from shutting on its own. I keep forgetting that I have a cover so I'll open the camera app and sit there like an idiot wondering why it's taking so long to load the viewfinder. Despite the case not being as thick as the camera bump, at least now the whole top is the same height so your phone doesn't wobble anymore on a flat surface. I recommend this case, it feels nice overall.

This case is striking in shiny red and black. It slips in and out of a back pocket VERY easily, maybe too easily. Buttons are covered, remain nice and easy to push and clicky. There is a good amount of depth at the camera for protection. I prefer a little bit more of a lip on the front, but there's enough lip to protect the screen when face down. No amount of lip will protect a screen that falls on an uneven surface.

The shiny red back shows fine scratches fairly easily, if you hold it in the light at a particular angle. That doesn't bother me. This case provides more protection than the slim TPU cases out there, I would say between light to moderate protection. It's easy to pop on the phone, a little hard to get off though (best done horizontally). That shiny back is slick, but the sides have some matte black rubber so that it's grippy enough to hold. Those sides also have glossy back that adds to the stylish look.

The inside has some cool little indentations. I'm not sure if that's for heat dissipation or shock absorption. There seem to be two fused layers, the inner black layer being a fairly hard rubber and the outer the hard plastic. Overall it's slim.

This would be good for people who want a slim case but don't want to completely give up on protection. Somehow I ran into it here on Amazon for $8.56. I think it's worth more than that. I've seen it at about $15 since then. This case isn't just thrown together by someone trying to make a quick buck. There was obviously some careful designing involved.

ArmorSuit has been my go to for screen protection from phones, watches, tablets. Customer service is top notch. I've had custom orders, they follow up with letter an extra protector. Fits S20 Ultra great.

All these years, I have fought with bubbles. I read a review about getting the matte one and that's what I chose. Not one air bubble. I do have one itty bitty nick (maybe it is air) but it is so small and you can't see it -but I can.

Truly terrific. I did not remove the entire backing since old habits die hard. I started from the bottom and placed it, removing the backing as I went along. I can tell that I should have positioned it slightly better (higher) but again no one will notice this but me and when I change my case again, I will know how to position it better.

I had no issues putting it on. I cleaned the screen with a dab of alcohol and a microfiber cleaning cloth. Some people said they went into the restroom to put it on - I put it on in the kitchen under the vent of my microwave. I put the fan on to make sure it pulled up any lint floating around.

I simply peeled part of it back and started at the bottom and I got it on without ANY bubbles.

I have a 5C phone and the screen protector did not go exactly edge to edge. I have a case on my phone and it is 100% protected.

Note: I wear both contacts and glasses but mainly glasses and, to me, the matte screen is perfect. There are no fingerprint smudges and I can see the display perfectly and I have my light set to under 50%. The screen felt weird at first, now it's normal and there is no change in sensitivity.

I am going to order the clear and/or glass one to compare when I change out my case.

This is a very pretty case. The "metallic foil accented design" catches the light creating a nice effect. The case is actually translucent so if you have a black phone, you'll have the pretty flower motif against the back of it and it won't look like the photo of the product. I wish they had included photos of the case on the other colors of the iPhone so you can see what it looks like on yours. I received Autumn Stock which is very pretty but doesn't look as cool as the Cranberry Sparkler.

Now that aesthetics are covered, we can discuss the material of the case. It feels very good in your hands. It's not slippery at all like I've found with similar cases. It's a sort of hard jelly case that doesn't bend as well. It's not as lightweight as the thinner jelly cases out there because of this co-molded style. You'll barely notice it on your phone on any case! The buttons are covered but I didn't have too much trouble using them while they were.

As far as I'm concerned, the price is not right. I think it's a little high for what you get. After all, for the same price, you can get a leather case from Otterbox or a folio case from Incipio and feel like you're getting more bang for your buck.

I found this protector fairly easy to apply. It came with the expected tools: cleaning wipes, a microfiber cloth, and dust tape. The later was the most useful. I only had a few small bubbles that I was able to squeeze out. While some parts of the screen along the edges remain uncovered, I don’t believe it’s an unreasonable amount. It fit well with all the cases I tried. I dropped my phone several times with this protector and it held up perfectly. It also lived up to the HD clear clarity I was expecting. It's also a cheaper alternative to the screen protector I usually use: [[ASIN:B00Z7SYN2G OtterBox ALPHA GLASS SERIES Screen Protector for iPhone X (ONLY) - Retail Packaging - CLEAR]].

Im coming from a Note 3 and ive had just about every phone you can imagine over the years. Recently my N3 broke so i had to go to Verizon and buy a phone, i read and read the reviews and finally decided to give the Icon a try.
First, the camera is not that great, indoor shots are terrible and grainy and outdoor shots are mediocre. Everyone in the reviews keeps saying how wonderful the cam is but compared to my S4 and Note 3 the pics are not as good.
The tiled menu interface is OK, i can get used to that but the lack of the apps in the app store is a real downer. The handful of popular apps they do have seem outdated and very badly written. For example, Facebook on this phone reminds of Facebook on the BlackBerry Bold, very clunky, things don't load fast and smooth, inability to do simple things that you take for granted with other operating systems, like "clicking like" on a pic.
The "People" stream/interface is horrid, it pulls in info from all over the place but when you click on a item it gives you a very bad/limited view of how to interact back.
The feel of the phone was nice and i have to say that the speakerphone and the call quality were superb, probably the best call quality i have heard in a long time on any phone so kudos to that.
Other things like having to go into settings, then into WIFI, to join a network is a pain, there should be a toggle switch somewhere for that but there isnt.
Im not saying this is a horrible phone but just my honest opinion of it. Anything i use that slows me down is just not for me, this phone completely slowed me down. Even the email formatting is a pain, to view images you have to click "view images" in every email, there is no default option to have it view images. Come on Microsoft....
Also, one last gripe, and the most important, the battery, its HORRIBLE. I charged the phone fully, pulled it off the charger at 7AM, never turned bluetooth on, had it on WIFI in my condo all day and by 7PM i was at 31%.
I would hate to seen the battery had i been out running around all day without a charger handy, it would have been dead by 2PM!k
Don't do what i did, this phone is shiny and looks real nice in the stores, but once you spend about a day with it i cant see how it would get a stellar review unless the ONLY thing you use it for is making calls, then it would deserve 5 stars.

This great looking battery pack is a nice way to give your device a little boost when on the go. It packs 5000 mAh to and supports fast charging too. I like the way it looks and built. It has a built-in kickstand so you can use it to prop up your device while it is charging. I also like that the cover for the connector is tethered to the case. I have lost these in the past and really like that this one is attached in some way. The LED indicator gives you a look at what percentage is left in the battery pack and what is even better is that it shows you what the percent it is as it charges, so you know exactly when you are fully charged at 100%.

All in all this is an excellent addition to anyone on the road to have as an emergency or back up power when you need to get you to the next outlet. It is competitively priced and the kickstand makes it a better option than most.

If you want some protection for your Note 9 and don't want to add a lot of bulk to what is already a big phone a rubber case like this is probably the way to go. What this one has going for it is that it's easy to put on and take off while still maintianing a firm grip on the phone and does a good job of protecting the rear cameras and the screen when you put the phone down on a surface. The cutouts and controls are also perfectly sized and there is enough of a cut-away on the sides to account for the Note's edge display. That being said this type of case doesn't provide tons of protection, they are meant to provide some pprotection against scratches while not adding a ton of weight or totally hiding the looks of the phone.

Update: This case is wonderful as I pointed out before, but it has one HUGE problem that's going to make me have to return it - it's almost impossible to take off without harming your phone because it fits so tightly! It took my wife and I over half an hour to finally pry it off. If you don't plan on removing the case it may still be the way to go, but if you're like me and switch between multiple phones stay away from this case since it must be removed to take out the sim card. It's a real shame since otherwise this is such a good case.

This case is fantastic - so long as you know exactly what it is for. The leather is super nice and the fit is fantastic. It is the only case I have ever used on the S22 Ultra that does not add to the bulk of the phone. In fact by making it "grippier" it makes the phone much easier to hold. However, this is not a case meant for full protection of the phone. There is a slight lip around the back cameras, so it should add a bit of protection there if you lay the phone down on it's back, and it covers all the sides so it will prevent scratches and scuffs on the back, but there is little, if any, protection of the front screen. It is also a very tight fit, so getting it off is a hassle. However, I have never seen a more elegant case, which is why I rated it a five. If you want a case that provides some protection while being very elegant this is the way to go.

Since 2012, I have owned the Galaxy Player 5.0 and still love it. Then I looked in the re-furbished ones on Amazon and found my 3.6 at a very reasonable price and ordered it (because I have bought FULFILLED by Amazon Sellers refurbished before with great success).
This player came to me looking BRAND NEW! And it works wonderfully, just as well at the Galaxy 5 player I have does!

PROS I FOUND USING THIS PLAYER:

1- WiFi connects easily and the signal is SUPERB in most places I tested. (Found the WiFi signal is VERY STRONG in my home and even in my backyard). This model has the strongest WiFi signal of any tablet or phone that I own! And it stays on the WiFi for long distances without dropping the signal EVER!
2- The advantage of having a SD card slot that will hold up to 32GB.
3- Small and lightweight. Comfortable to carry around and place in backpack or briefcase easily.
4- Screen clarity - For me, I found this screen to be very sharp with vivid colors!
5- Peppy! This is a peppy as my other player and also found it to be peppy and very responsive!
6- Every app I installed works wonderfully!
7) Love the music player on this, easy to make new playlists, etc.
8) Bluetooth works well - I paired this to my Bluetooth speakers, my Bluetooth headset with no problems and the music I played sounds great!

Suggestions:

1) I went into the Playstore and bought FREE Fancy Widgets, this is a clock/weather widget that is outstanding (the player comes with a clock but no weather widget).

2) Most cases (with belt clips) for a 4 inch phone will fit this. I have a case that I used in my old Galaxy S phone and the device fits like a glove in it.

If you are to have any questions about this player, you may ask me in the comment section and I will answer right away.

I have a gold 7 Plus with 128 GB. After having used Android phones since Android first came out, I decided that I wanted to see what an iPhone was like, just for a change. I had an LG V10, and while the phone itself was OK, I wasn't thrilled with the latest Android update, so that's another reason I switched. Also, I have an iMac and wanted to have a phone that could share the calendar, photos, and more.

I chose to get the 7 Plus rather than the 8 Plus because, after comparing the two, I just couldn't see paying more for something that isn't much different from the 7 Plus. I've had the phone for about a week now, and all I can say is, I wish I had switched to an iPhone much sooner. I really love this phone! I haven't used all the features that are available, and will update when I've discovered more.

Pros:
1. It's very user-friendly. I thought that I would have a big learning curve switching from Android, but I didn't. Maybe that's because I already had an Apple product, but even if I hadn't, I don't think it would be that difficult, especially since I didn't have much difficulty switching from a PC to a Mac.
2. The dual speakers are wonderful. I've had many different brands of phones, and the speakers on this one are finally loud enough to where I can have a phone conversation with them on, and hear perfectly.
3. It quickly paired with my LG Tone Infinim headphones, and my 2012 Buick LaCrosse.
4. It has a wonderful camera, and the pictures are much clearer and sharper than my V10, which had a pretty good camera.
5. Siri is definitely the best assistant out there, and I'm going to use her a lot.
6. I like the fact that one can still assign photos and specific ringtones to contacts.
7. It had no problem accepting Amazon Prime Video and Music.
8. The clock, if used as an alarm, is great. However, it has one quirk. If you want to use the snooze feature, you can't leave the phone on. You don't turn the phone completely off, but you do lock it with a quick click of the power button. I find that kind of strange, but that's how Apple decided to do it.
9. I have small hands, and it feels comfortable to hold. My V10 was a hair larger, so this phone seems a bit better comfort-wise.
10. Apple Support is awesome!

Cons:

1. I hate the home button. It's really irritating to have to press that thing instead of swiping left or right when I want to access something. In this respect, they're way behind Android. The only other phone I had with a home button was a Samsung S5, and I never liked it. I'm glad Apple has done away with it, and will probably trade mine in for an 8 Plus next year.
2. The battery is pretty awful. I don't use my phone all that much, and that thing drains pretty quickly. I can't imagine what it would do if I were a heavy user, complete with videos and games.
3. As another reviewer pointed out, the head phones that come with the phone are very uncomfortable, so I'll use either Bluetooth or use the adapter with some that I already have.

That's all I can think of for negatives. If I find any more, I'll add them in an update.

A FEW WORDS ABOUT WARRANTIES:

I already have an extended warranty on the phone via AT&T. However, I've been asking lots of questions about Apple Care, and may still get that. I was told by an Apple Support person that I should keep the Asurion one because Apple doesn't cover it if it's lost or stolen. I've only had one cell phone stolen, but there could be a next time, especially since a lot of thieves like the iPhone because it keeps its resale value longer.

However, with Apple Care, if I have a problem with my phone, they'll send me an immediate replacement, even before they get my old one. Of course, I have to give a credit card number just in case they don't get the old one back.

Bottom line, do some in-depth research regarding extra coverage for this phone. It's an expensive investment, and you don't want to get stuck having to pay full-price for another one if something goes wrong.

I had a plastic phone mount that came with my Joby Gorillapod, but my new iPhone 6 Plus doesn't fit, so I needed something different. When I stumbled upon this metal one, I was very excited to try it out.

One of my fears with the plastic mount was that it felt delicate - like it wanted to break at the most inopportune time. That fear is no more with this beautifully machined aluminum mount. Sure, it's bigger and heavier than the plastic one (which even folded flat), but this metal one is tough to beat. In today's world where cheap plastic is abundant, it's nice to see a product with good, solid, quality, metal construction.

I also like that rather than having a spring, which can wear out, this mount employs a simple thumb-screw to adjust the size and lock the mount onto the phone. There's virtually nothing to wear out with this and I have no doubt that it will provide years of reliable service.

The rubber pads protect your phone from damage while in this mount (just don't go nuts torquing the thumbscrew too much - it's not necessary for a secure hold). This case has an excellent implementation for protecting the front screen (that's the one you see when the phone is folded).
Almost all other cases have but a thin plastic strip that goes around the front screen, and they put it there to match the case for the rest of the phone. It provides little protection, if that. Some similar cases (for the front half) include a bit of adhesive on the inner sides, so it sticks to the phone and not fall off. Sorta like the phone providing some rigidity to the case.
What Ruky has done is to build in the front screen protector into the front surround, which does two good things: you get a pre-fitted screen protector, and second, it makes the plastic surround much more structured, so it can actually do a bit of protecting.
The rest of the product, the back half, if you will, is fairly standard, and probably identical to all other cases in the market.
What I did was to take the front half of this product, and the back half of the OEM case (which has the built-in stand), and put them on my fold 4. They look like a set, I get my front screen protection and case for the front half, and a case+stand for the rest.
Works very well.

Upon install with its frame guide you need to smooth over ALL sides making sure that there are NO gaps to prevent dust and particles from getting in. This is very important if you always put your phone in your pants' pocket(s).

(1) Installation was a breeze. Guide Frame is provided.

(2) It is DRY install. So it's clean. No spray bottles needed. No mess.

(3) Prior to install, make SURE your screen is squeeky CLEAN and DRY.

PROTECTION:

(1) I had the screen protector over the course of 7 months. And the only noticeble scratch occurred when I mistakenly put my phone on my lap and it slid down the ground filled with gravel.

(2) I'm pretty rough on my phone's screens. Always accidentally dropping them or having it slide down as I have the habit of putting it on my lap, standing up, and not realizing it was there. It happened numerous times and the protector remained intact.

(3) It finally cracked (as my habit described above) when it slipped and WHAM BAM slammed on a hard rough concrete. (SEE PHOTOS)

(4) I took the protector off, crossing my fingers -- and whoala! The iPhone screen remained in good condition!

I'm glad they provide (3) protectors in the package.

I'll be buying this again as I've already used all of them within the course of the year; as again I'm pretty rough on my screens.

This is one of the best cases to get out there for the Motorola Edge (USA version NOT the Edge+ version; though I think their size are the same).

It fits well all around. It has a slight lip around the top and bottom of the screen for slam proof protection and compatible with tempered glass or film protectors.

The cut-outs are all well placed: power button, bottom firing speakers, USB-C port, microphones headphone jack. The camera cut-out is slightly raised for surface protection.

The volume rockers are covered and very tactile.

The construction is matte and feels good on the hand. It has a faux carbon fiber design at the bottom half of the case.

If I have to fault this case is that it’s slightly smooth, making it difficult to handle in one hand.

The screen protector that came with it is TPU (not tempered). I do prefer this as I’ve tried a few tempered glass and they never provide good touch feedback, often disabling fingerprint touch capabilities.

The screen protector didn’t come with instructions, although I was able to put it with moderate ease. Almost all the bubbles, came out 12 hours after install. It’s very clear and tactile. It’s only for scratch protection and won’t protect the screen like tempered glass do if you drop it on a hard concrete.

Overall, I would recommend this case for someone who likes slim and unobstructed cases.

Professional reviews for phones are usually the best resource. However, that said, I want to point out a couple of things from those professional reviews that turned out to be untrue in my experience. In general, I think the reviewers were too harsh. Some of them even had me on the brink of canceling my order. Glad I didn't.

I have the 64GB XL.

Battery life - I am not seeing much difference from my Pixel 3. The horrible battery life mentioned in some reviews is not my experience. I consider myself an average smartphone user. I'm not a 16 year old on social media all day and I don't play games. I use it all day at work (email, text, in car for music, reading some blogs with videos, the occasional call) and a little at night. When I get home, I have over 50% left. I keep the always-on display on, I use motion sense, all the connection methods are on. The key here is that I don't see a big difference from my past phones.

Also - you have to give the Android OS time to use its adaptive battery function to manage various aspects of the phone. It learns your usage patterns and adjusts. This results in better battery life. I saw many reviews where the author didn't give it time.

HD Calling on AT&T - I bought this unlocked model on Amazon and when I swapped my SIM out, HD calling works normally. There might be an issue with corporate accounts but search the web and look at what some people did to circumvent it. This is an AT&T issue, not Google's.

Motion Sense - no, it doesn't have a lot of active functionality yet, only track skipping and alarm snoozing (which is AWESOME). What many reviews fail to highlight enough is that it augments the face unlock feature by bringing your phone to attention when you reach for it. It also allows you to turn off the always-on display then have it wake only when you are near the phone.

New assistant - pretty cool. Many of the basic requests are now handled on the phone, not requiring contact with Google servers. It really speeds things up.

As of Nov 1, it does have little things that I hope Google works out. Face Unlock needs to be updated to prevent unlocks when your eyes are closed. It would be nice if Smooth Display (90Hz) worked more often - although, I think the display is awesome as is, it can only get better. Google is planning to fix those. More apps need to support face unlock and that's a total wildcard; depends on the app. LastPass supports it so that's sort of a gateway to your other apps.

**UPDATE 11/10/2019** The phone received an update that activated "Smooth Display" 90Hz refresh rate across more conditions. It's on much more often. The display is stunning. Also, more apps already support Face Unlock such as Citi, Bank of America, and USAA.

Finally, you can't deny this is an expensive phone. I got it for, basically, $100 off and that brought it closer to an acceptable price for me. But - it's a personal decision. You'll have to weigh the value and decide if it works for you.

If you are considering this phone, I'll be honest, also consider the Pixel 3XL (or Pixel 3). Many of the new features on the 4 will make it to the 3. If you don't need to be on the cutting edge or the extra camera features, the 3/3XL is a great phone! However, you won't be disappointed by the 4XL. After a week of getting used to face unlock and the gestures, I really like this phone.

NOTE ON MEMORY - My last two phones were 64GB. Prior to that, I bought 128GB. With my past two phones, I never ran out of space with 64GB. I keep several large music playlists downloaded, I upload all my images to Google Photos and delete them from my phone.

I wasn’t going to write a review but I decided to because a lot of people aren’t giving this phone a chance. I also recall how the Pixel 4XL was trashed before anyone gave it a chance. So, here’s a real review after three weeks with Pixel 6 Pro.

BATTERY LIFE: Very good! You need to let the adaptive battery smarts learn how you use the phone and start adjusting power use as needed. I was worried on day one but now, very satisfied. This is my experience but give it a 2-3 days and see if yours is the same. It’s not stellar battery performance but it’s very good.

FINGERPRINT SENSOR: This is a complicated one. I hated it at first. I thought Google made a huge mistake. First, you need to stay out of developer options and don’t change the animation scales. Once you do this, the lock screen will consistently show you the fingerprint symbol. Turn on the extra touch sensitivity in the phone’s options. Enroll your finger(s) twice (advice given to many different branded phones). When enrolling your prints, make sure you hold the phone in your hand like you normally would when using the sensor. From there, you need to learn how to use it. It takes some pressure, go ahead and give it solid press. I kept missing the center but with time, muscle memory developed and now I am satisfied with the FPS. It isn’t the greatest but it’s also not “terrible” or “unusable.” It’s a fraction of a second slower than the one that was on my Galaxy S21. I don’t think this should stop you from enjoying this phone. I am getting close to not even noticing anything unusual with the FPS. Time heals all… FPS issues – well, most.

There was a mid-November update issued that helps improve the FPS experience. Make sure you check for updates when you get the phone. It will check automatically during set up but check again just in case.

CHARGING: This is one that I don’t have a fix for and I think Google mislead people on the phone’s ability to fast charge. It does use high-speed charging to get to 50% and that’s what they promised. Unfortunately, 51% to 100% is closer to regular rapid charging. You won’t get super-fast charging from 50% to 100%. I can live with it since the phone easily makes it through my day. I just charge my phone overnight. It has a nice adaptive charging feature to protect your battery when overnight charging. I think (double-check) you need a brick that can deliver at least 23 watts.

THE CAMERA BAR: Before I got the phone, I thought it was going to be problematic, easy to scratch. Well, the opposite. I love it. It looks cool and the phone does not rock when you have it on a table (unlike S21 and Apple iPhone). My case has a lip around it. I am trying out a camera lens protector because they came in the box with my screen protectors. Not sure I need it. My case seems to protect it on its own.

SCREEN PROTECTOR: Unfortunately, on the Pixel 6 Pro, you are mostly stuck with TPU film protectors. They get dirty easily and wear out much sooner than tempered glass. There’s also all this talk about how they affect fingerprint performance. My film does not have a fingerprint sensor problem, but it has typical dirty and wear issues. Since I am picky about the screen, I will probably end up replacing the film soon. This isn’t a Pixel problem per se, it’s TPU film protectors. Also, note that the regular Pixel 6 screen does not have the rolled edges, it’s squared off so you can get tempered glass for it.

CAMERA: This is obvious. It’s fantastic. The 4x optical Zoom is so cool. Magic Eraser is neat but doesn’t always work well. I’m not a pro photographer. I am probably like you, someone who enjoys everyday photos. The Pixel is doing a GREAT job with my everyday photo experience. It does a whole heck of a lot more that I still need to explore.

MATERIAL YOU THEMING: I thought this was going to be silly. It’s not. It’s kinda neat the way it pulls colors out of your wallpaper and uses them throughout the phone. I like it!

OTHER THINGS TO CONSIDER: No way around it, this is a big phone. Check it out in person if you are concerned. And it’s slippery! Buy a case when you buy the phone. Look at what the regular Pixel 6 has to offer and decide if the Pro is worth it for you

I came from a Galaxy S21+ and I am really enjoying all of the Pixel features. I love the call screen and “hold for me.” The high resolution, adaptive high refresh rate make for a beautiful screen experience. This is the phone to get if you want Google’s latest tech. They just added “Adaptive Sound” recently! Yes, an update… a quiet little update.

When you read other reviews, which you should, make sure the reviewer isn’t expressing only first day, gut reactions. Make sure they have used the phone for at least several days.

After using the phone for three weeks and admittedly being a Pixel/Android person, I give it 4.5 stars. The half star off is for that charging discrepancy. It’s a great phone that you will enjoy. You are probably going to be married to your phone for at least a year so read other reviews, not just mine.

Photos: My Pixel 6 Pro (Cloudy White) in the Spigen Thin Fit case. Lock screens. The phone in the fantastic light frost Google case.

*UPDATE 6 MONTHS IN* I took a star off because it's still a little bit buggy. The last update did improve everything but mine just froze today and became unresponsive for a minute. I love everything else about this phone. I hope that was a fluke and I can enjoy the phone without freezes or crashes.

*UPDATE below

The camera protector is EXTREMELY difficult to remove. The screen protector is fine.

The screen protector film worked like most others I have used. Any small bubbles did disappear overnight. The fingerprint reader works pretty well. Installation was not difficult (watch the video). Picks up smudges a bit more easily than others but it is also easy to wipe off.

However, DO NOT PUT THE CAMERA PROTECTOR ON unless you plan to leave it on for the entire time you own the phone. It is extremely difficult to remove. When you actually start getting it to lift up, the protector's glass cracks up into shards (like a window might). Then you have to meticulously pick off each piece being careful not to scratch or damage the cameras. This process ripped up my nails because the adhesive is so strong. For the record, yes, I used a hair dryer to try and soften the adhesive.

Maybe you see that as a good thing, it sure not to fall off. I see it as a negative. I have had others that lift off with a little pressure and stay in one piece (and never fall off on their own).

To sum it up - screen protectors are very good, and camera protector is permanent (nearly).

I'm just going to go without a camera protector and use cases with good camera bar protection.

PHOTO: The remnants I could find. Oddly, it seems like some of it is missing. I have to go back and vacuum the area to get the little bits.

*UPDATE* The company (Milomdoi) contacted me to see if there was anything they could do to help which is a huge plus. There wasn't much they could do but I appreciate the offer. I have purchased this brand of screen protector in the past for two other phones. I want to repeat: The screen protectors themselves are very good. Stay away from the camera protectors.

Having owned a Galaxy Tab 2 7" tablet for quite awhile now I wasn't too scared to ditch my old iPhone 4 and decided to give the Moto X a shot after reading some good reviews and seeing it offered for free. I loved my iPhone, but wasn't too thrilled with ios7 and didn't care for spending $200 on a new iPhone. I figured if I didn't like the Moto X then I'd reactive the old iPhone or suck it up and spend the money on a new one.
So far I'm pretty happy with this phone. To keep it simple I'll give you my list of Pros & Cons:

Pros:
Nice size: It has a larger screen than my old iPhone, but it's still light and fits in my pocket nicely. I didn't want a phablet
Works well: I think having too much "stuff" on top of Android can be a bad thing. No glitches here it just all works
Google Voice: This works much better than I expected and seems better than Siri does on my wife's iPhone
Motorola Migrate: It was pretty darn easy to go from iPhone to Android. I got my Contacts and Music easily.
Android: There are some nice customizable features that Android has. I'm too lazy to get into it, but you can do some cool things

Cons:
Voice Mail: iPhone has this nicely laid out, but I have to dial *86 here? WTF did I go back in time? Luckily the Google Voice app is as good if not better, but why do I have to download an app just for voicemail? And Verizon charges for Visual VoiceMail? Seriously?
Camera: If you take a lot of pics then skip this phone. It's "good enough", but most phones now take better pics
E-mail: Links in some e-mails don't work. I'm sure there's some setting to fix this, but I haven't found it yet. Just another little thing that isn't a big deal, but wouldn't even be a problem with an iPhone.

That's it really. It's a solid capable phone. Too bad Google ditched Motorola because the next generation might've been really, really good. I still think these phones need to be a bit better set up out of the box like iPhone, but they're getting there. Had I paid $100 or $200 for this phone I'd be disappointed only with the camera and voicemail because otherwise everything works like it should and the size is right for me.

Right after I ordered this, I saw a couple of video reviews on youtube from MobileReviewsEh and they had a lot of negative things to say about the case.

What I discovered when mine showed up was that the things they were complaining about, my case had none of. The rubber sleeve is thick and spongy and VERY VERY tight on the phone. There are no loose parts anywhere. I could use just the rubber sleeve and be perfectly happy knowing that this thing could survive a hard drop.

The plastic outer shell is also pretty hard to get on just right and get everything lined up but it seems that it is this way only because of how tight the tolerances are for the fit. Once I got all the rubber sleeve edges tucked in so the outer plastic shell "Claw" parts that wrap around the side of the device were lined up properly, everything fit incredibly well.

The phone feels amazingly well protected, the case feels much more durable than the Defender on my wife's iPhone 4s which fell apart pretty quickly and the case looks nice. I like that the apple logo and camera have a beveled edge around their openings.

The flap for the lightning port and headphone jack are large enough that you can use 3rd party lightning cables with larger connectors (the Thule case doesn't allow for this) and for the headphone jack, you can use standard non stepped 3.5mm jacks as well as your average right angle jack. Again, not something that the Thule case I also bought lets you do.

If I had one negative thing to say, it would be that the plastic outer shell does make the phone a bit slick in the hands. The rubber edge lip that protects the screen though is always in your hand so gripping that helps keep the phone in your hand when using with 1 hand.

Anyways, this case has everything I was after. It's got solid protection, amazingly well fitting and doesn't sacrifice aesthetics to offer that protection like several other rugged cases. Highly recommended.

This is not a big deal-just take the case off and clean the back of the phone and the case. It also seems like this case will withstand a drop. If I changed anything; it would be to put a stand in the back. Not a big deal. The screen protector that came for the front?! I cut it a bit and put it on the back of my phone! I have a glass screen protector on the front of the phone; so with that and this case; I feel quite protected. And did I say it looks cool?! Highly recommend-I don't have to be excessively careful holding on to this case because it is grippy! With any other case I have; I am tenuously holding on to the phone; not so with this case-cannot emphasize that enough!

The holster appears to have a spring type mechanism-seems sturdy. It does flip out on the volume rocker side but no big deal. I ordered the white model-very pretty but like almost all white cases, a bit slippery. The case does have the black rubber pieces on sides-down a bit but good enough to have a grip piece so you don't drop the case. Nice lip on front-didn't mess with my black screen protector either. Great to have a substantial holster-I have to wear a black brace so can hook it on that. I tend to carry my phone around the house-sometimes with other items and the holster solves the problem of potentially dropping other things you might be carrying! Overall a 5-star item! All right today what I have for everyone is my full written review of the Sony Xperia I Mark II. I've decided to break this review down into the positives and negatives of the device. then I'm going to talk about the overall battery life and charge times. Lastly we'll wrap everything up talking about the pricing my final thoughts and I'll make an overall recommendation. So without further Ado let's begin.

Positives-starting off talking about all the positives that this device offers. It has an overall really solid build quality and design. with a really overall balanced weight and really nice feel in the hands. It has an overall solid core feature set with every feature you would expect to see in a premium flagship device so wireless charging waterproofing micro SD card expansion headphone jack etc etc. It also has some really good dual front-facing speakers with some really good overall volume and clarity it also has one of the best displays that I've ever experienced on a smart device especially with that 21x9 cinematic ratio display. lastly it has some really good cameras. Okay I believe that about does it for all the positives let's move on.
Negatives-talking about the negatives on this device there are really only three that I came across in my time with it the first one is the brightness of the display outdoors it really doesn't get that bright even at Max brightness outdoors I still had trouble seeing it especially in direct sunlight period so that definitely needs some improvement another negative has to be the focusing speeds of the cameras in anything less than perfect light or perfect lighting is really bad there's no other way I can say it so that definitely also needs some improvement. And the last negative of this device would have to be the price that it's available for for that price which is something we're going to talk about at the end of the review you would expect everything to be perfect and sadly I was a little disappointed. That being said perfection is something that just doesn't exist so it is what it is.
Battery life & overall charge times-talking about the overall battery life on this device it was really good top notch in my opinion I easily was able to get through a 12 to 14 hour work day on a single charge and in terms of sot or screen on time I easily averaged between 9 and 11 hours of screen on time depending on what I was doing. That to me is top-notch battery life in my opinion especially considering that I am what people would consider a heavy user when it comes to using smartphones. Now talking about the charge times it was just as good on average this device starts to full 100%, in about an hour and 50 minutes and it turns to 50%, and just under half an hour so in terms of charge times I would have to say it's top-notch in my opinion as well and the same rules apply for wireless charging it on average took between 3 to 4 hours to reach it full charge. All right that about sums up my experience with that let's move on to the final part of the review.
Price and final thoughts-okay so in terms of pricing this device is available ranging anywhere from $950 all the way up to $1199. Now this pricing is on par with premium flagships today. So with that being said if you were wondering if I can recommend this product I would have to say yes but only if you're in the market for a premium flagship device and you can deal with the negatives that I pointed out if you can deal with that then I do believe this device will serve you well. That being said if this device is well outside of your price range there are other devices that can do everything this device can do at a third of the price so it really comes down to what you're willing to spend or what you can afford. All right I think that about does it for this one again I hope everyone found this helpful and I hope everyone has a good day evening and or night depending on the time and your location when you're reading this review.

Original Title:
When You Factor In Cost (& Some Of Its Neat Capabilities), Lack Of Refinement Is A Problem Maybe Worth Overlooking

Original Review:
If I were to rate this phone strictly on quality and refinement I think we're looking at a 3-star product. However, price plays a really important role here so once price is factored in I think this is a solid 4-star product.

In many ways this is a really nice (maybe, even great) phone. But it is rough in a number of areas. The analogy that kept coming to mind for me was like comparing a refined Toyota to a first generation Kia; they're both cars but they are worlds apart in terms of refinement.

For instance, the screen is absolutely stunning (especially if you use the gold/black pre-installed "matrix" photo for the gold edition of the phone. But the Android overlay is basic and unrefined. The contrast between the MiFavor (?) apps "skin" and the supplied HD background images and display displaying them is - to me - jarring.

Sound is every bit as good as promised; really, really nice. Just as some manufacturers tout the camera or company-produced apps, ZTE can rightly claim an excellent audio experience; the front speakers (at top & bottom of the phone) produce really nice sound (how good the audio jack sound is will, of course, depend more on your headphones than the phone). How it stacks up to everything out there is something I can't say, but I can say it stood up well to a Samsung Note 5, a Samsung GS7, an LG G5, and a Moto G 4.

Performance is good; basic apps function without a problem and I did not feel the phone bogged down when dealing with audio and video files. No problem running a Verizon SIM, a T-Mobile SIM, or an ATT&T SIM. Unlike the Moto G 4 Amazon offers, it doesn't support Sprint (this is only a problem if you're wedded to Sprint; this phone is FAR superior to the Moto G 4). I did not try the dual-SIM capability (I might down the road) as I use the second SIM/SD card slot for an SD card (which btw, I've been told supports at least 128GB (I've only used up to a 64GB card)).

Not loaded down with bloatware.

The camera isn't bad, either; absolutely fine for everyone but the most "photogs" of folks.

I won't comment on cell phone reception as I'm finding this is really more about the provider than the phone. Bottom line, it works like a phone should (!), but results will certainly vary depending on your carrier, the distance to nearest tower, geography, etc.

I am not a fan, however, of the fingerprint reader on the back of the phone. Seems gimmicky. Unlike the LG G5 (and others?; where the "home button" and fingerprint reader are integrated on the back side of the phone) or the Samsung or Apple or whoever else (where the "home button" and fingerprint reader are integrated together in the more-expected front, bottom location) this splits the functions into two locations. To my mind, it seems ZTE just wants to be different. But I also must confess that my love affair with fingerprint readers is fading (so I may just be being picky here); if what I read in various security/legal blogs is true no search warrant is necessary if a phone is locked via a fingerprint while one is if the phone is locked with a passphrase or passcode (has something to do with a fingerprint being no different than what is found at a crime scene where no permission is necessary to collect it (or something like that)).

There's also a lack of choice when it comes to accessories. Yes, there are several cases and screen protectors available, but none of the "big players" seem to be playing yet. So I bought a Speck-like soft case ([[ASIN:B01ITTPNS2 ZTE Axon 7 Case, CoverON® [ClearGuard Series] Hard Clear Back Cover with Flexible TPU Bumpers Slim Fit Phone Cover Case for ZTE Axon 7 - Black]]) and a film screen protector ([[ASIN:B01GQWY49S ZTE Axon 7 Screen Protector [2-Pack], DeltaShield BodyArmor Full Coverage Screen Protector for ZTE Axon 7 Military-Grade Clear HD Anti-Bubble Film - Lifetime Warranty]]) and am going with that. For me, that takes away from the overall feel & look. And finally, speaking of feel and look, I really don't like the gold scheme. I know gold is a popular color, but not for me; I wish I had the opportunity to see/feel the gray or black models. I bring this up because I can't help but wonder if my dislike of the color is influencing my review of the phone. I would like to think it isn't, but....

In the end, reviewers I've read here on Amazon and else on the inter webs aren't kidding: this phone is a decent entry into the unlocked phone field. Put it in your hand and you won't confuse it with a GS7 or an iPhone. But your wallet won't be confused either: at about 2/3 the cost of these premium cell phones you get really good performance at a really good price (though, as I've mentioned, you're not getting a gem in terms of refinement (both hardware (and when I say "hardware" I'm referring to how the physical product looks/feels) or Android build)). Speaking of Android , I wonder if this phone would be considered a "rooter's"/"modder's" dream? This is (not Taiwan) China's attempt to bust into the U.S. cell phone market as a stand-alone manufacturer and not a manufacturer of other firm's hardware. It's a good start. Whether giving money directly to a Chinese company is a political question the prospective buyer gets to mull over; this review seeks to focus on whether that discussion even needs to be had.

Summary: if you're evaluating strictly on terms of refinement (hard- (again, how the phone looks/feels) and software), 3 stars. If price matters, add a star ('cause you can live with some rough edges and some of its capabilities (sound, dual SIM, etc.) makeup for what it lacks (refined skin, dubious design choices, etc.)).

The Otterbox Defender series has been a favorite of mine since I bought one for my HTC Evo 4G a number of years back. Since then I've had Defender series cases on my Kindle Fires, iPads and iPods, and other phones. Frankly, while there are cheaper "defender"-type case out there, I have yet to find one that does the job as well as the Otterbox one does.

The Otterbox Defender series is a complete solution in terms of protection. First, the case is a plastic shell that is enclosed in a rubber outer layer with tabs to cover the charging port. Not intended to be water-proof, but it is certainly water- and dirt-resistant. Protection extends to the built-in screen protector that keeps dust, dirt, and grime out of the case and away from the phone. The inner plastic shell snaps together tightly and seamlessly so if dirt gets past the outer rubber layer it still has the plastic shell to get by. Finally, the Defender series has a holster where the clip rotates 360 degrees (giving the user the benefit of a variety of options), as well as a curved clip bottom (so that the clip positively locks on the belt; it can't slide off). It seems to me that this holster has gone forward virtually unchanged since at least back when I had my Evo (and before) because why mess with something that works (and works well)?

But the Defender case is also the "biggest" in the Otterbox line. It will (perhaps, literally) "double the size" of your phone (in terms of thickness) and I know some will find that off-putting. It's funny how going for a "complete" protection package negates one reason why many buy the latest smart phone: sleek size. Anyway, Otterbox offers the Commute and Symmetry cases if this increase in size is annoying, but if "max protect" is what you're after this Defender case is the way to go.

It's not a "perfect" case, though; there are two things that really bug me. First, the integrated plastic screen protector I had loved so much now seems cheap with the introduction of glass screen protectors. Wish Otterbox could figure a way to upgrade this to glass. And second, while the case protects the Apple logo Otterbox still hasn't figured out a way to protect the camera lens (did I mention "glass"?).

I had the opportunity to look at the black, "Bespoke Way" ("blazer blue"/"stormy seas blue"), "Borealis" ("tempest blue/"aqua mint"), "Realtree Max 5HD" (blaze orange/black/Max 5 Design), "Realtree Xtra" (blaze orange/black/Xtra Design), and "Realtree Xtra Pink" (white/gunmetal/Xtra Pink Design). Black is, well, black. The "Bespoke Way" and "Borealis" are eye-catching. The Realtree schemes are nice, too (Realtree Xtra and Realtree Xtra Pink are very, very nice; wasn't as thrilled with the Realtree Max 5HD). Prices are very competitive (except for the non-Realtree schemes): $27.66 (black), $31.95 ("Bespoke Way"), $28.00 ("Borealis"), $59.95 ("Realtree Max 5HD"), $44.99 ("Realtree Xtra"), and $53.96 (Amazon prices at the time of this review). (For whatever it's worth, of the ones I've had the opportunity to review the Realtree Xtra is by far my favorite.)

Know that if you do choose to use this case (or any Otterbox case) Otterbox customer service/tech support is as awesome as it's always been (they have an excellent historical record with supporting warranty claims, listening to customer concerns, and making good on the promises they make - at least in my experience).

4-5 stars. Rounded down to 4 stars (tempted, though, to round up to 5 stars because I love the Realtree Xtra scheme). While I LOVE the Defender series, there is one thing that is getting a little long in the tooth (plastic screen protector) and one thing that needs getting fixed (camera lens protection).

This was a cheap case and it broke after having it for not that long. I would recommend a silicone one instead.

I honestly had better expectations for this case even considering what other reviewers said about it. I knew it would be nice and thin but it is like PAPER THIN. Even just flexing it in your hand could cause rips on the edges. I would have returned it except this item does not do free returns, so it would cost me almost as much to return it as just keeping it, so it’s currently collecting dust in a drawer now. I like thin cases but I want something a little stronger than just paper thin.

I had been looking for a semi-stylish hard case for my phone. Unlike most people, I wanted a case that DIDN'T have a "grippy" rubberized texture. I have had problems in the past with cases with "matte coatings" for grip because the material seems to age over time and become stickier and sticker. This attracts more and more dust coming out of my pocket, and makes the overall phone look cheap and tacky.

And so, the Incipio Esquire series is exactly the type of case that I was looking for. It is still made of plastic, but it is just textured and colored in a way to resemble fabric. According to Incipio's customer support, it is made of "Plextonium", a material that apparently is more resistant to wearing from sun exposure, salt, and other secretions normally found in a person's hand. These are the same reasons why rubberized matte finish cases age and get stickier and excessively grippy over time.

Keep in mind, this case neither has a strong grip nor super slippery either. The textured fabric like design is what provides the grip in the hand, NOT the material. The texture would feel something along the lines or a pair of jeans or a sweater. The edges do have a thin strip of black plastic. It's just where the fabric design ends. The top and bottom edges do have a slight lip to it for a lay on the table design. And the case is also thin enough to support wireless charging.

Note: The case IS very tight. If you are someone who likes to remove your case often, then this could be rather annoying for you. But if you just want a thin and simple case to cover up the nude glass black of your phone, then this could be a good option for you. It's not going to be the best drop protection, but for simple scuffs and scratches putting them on tables, etc. This would be a superb case.

I would preferred more color options like in the previous S8 model, preferably a darker shade. But Incipio has informed me that the S9 version is made from a different material and thus only comes in 2 colors. It's not a big enough deal for me to dock a star off the review, so the 5 star rating stands. So far, the case is working fine. If I have problems in the future, I may edit this review to put extra notes.

Update: 6/24/2018
After using it for a short while, I am starting to notice that the fabric areas of the case is starting to fray. The border area closest to the matte black part of the case seems to be the biggest trouble areas. I feel like for such light use of the product so far, there should not be ANY damage whatsoever. I have only ever kept it in my pocket or rested on a table. So, I am not sure if I got a defective product or not. I am currently waiting for an response for the trouble ticket I put in at Incipio's main website. I will continue to update this review accordingly. In the meantime, I will have to dock off 2 stars for the major drop in quality thus far.

Update: 7/7/2018
Contacted Incipio Support by email twice only to get automated responses telling me that they would process my ticket within 3-5 business days. Got sick of waiting for almost two weeks and decided to call in personally to talk to a rep. The rep stated that I needed to send in pictures of the case to show the damage and then they would be able to process a replacement. I sent in clear pictures of all the damage within 20 minutes. Waited for about a week then decided to call in just to ask about my support ticket. Turns out no replacement was sent out at all yet because they didn't even process my ticket yet. They claim they are really busy now and haven't gotten around to my ticket yet. But once it is processed, an order would be placed and they would ship my replacement out. Frankly, while I like the design of the case, its quality/durability and the customer service speed of Incipio isn't up to proper quality standards. I'm knocking off 2 more stars because of several weeks of waiting and STILL the replacement isn't even processed to even be shipped at all yet. If I knew it was going to be this much trouble, I would've just gotten a replacement through Amazon instead. Amazon processes damaged replacements and ships them out within a day...

If Incipio doesn't ship my replacement within the next week, I think I will be contacting Amazon instead to file a complaint. I have no issues forwarding all the emails I have sent and received so far with the timestamps clearly shown, which clearly shows that I have made more than adequate attempts to contact Incipio for service AND waited a considerable amount of time for them to process a replacement.

Update: 8/9/2018
After weeks of emails, then weeks more for the shipping time, I finally received my replacement case today and was totally disappointed. Incipio had sent me the wrong color. Simply unbelieveable! I double checked my emails with them and confirmed that I had given them the right amazon order number, the right product name WITH gray in the title, and even mentioned that I am reporting an issue with my "gray" case. So, there should not have been any reason to send me a blue case as my replacement.

I am done with Incipio now. Their case and replacement will be going directly in the garbage. I am not about to deal with another month worth of waiting, going from multiple emails back and forth, to order processing, to delivery time, just to get it in my hands on a proper replacement. I couldn't even if I wanted to because I will be moving to a different country abroad next weekend, and Incipio has already stated that they CANNOT deliver to that country. So, I am basically out $35.

I simply CANNOT recommend Incipio cases to anyone now. Not only was their case quality very questionable with the easy fraying over light use, their warranty replacement customer service experience has been horrid with slow responses, order processing, and wait times. I would suggest that people complain directly to amazon and get the replacement shipped out directly from amazon instead. You'd get it shipped within days and never have to deal with the lengthy Incipio support process. For the price that you pay for the case, you'd think that you should get a 5 star quality product and excellent customer support, but I unfortunately received the conplete opposite of that. Who knows if anyone else had a different experience. But at least for me, it's just too much pain and effort for what is supposed to be just a simple case for a phone. Not worth it at all! So if you are a fan of Incipio, I'd excercise caution for "this" case. Perhaps they have many other products that are excellent, but "this" one, has been a totally horrible experience.

Update: 8/15/2018
Just got an email from Incipio. They stated that they purposely just sent out a replacement of whatever random color they had available and ignored what original color I had. Their reasoning was since my color was out of stock, having any case was better than nothing. They said that if I wanted to, I could send them back the case (with my own money), and they'd send me the proper color when it comes back in stock (who knows when that'd be). The question is: Why didnt Incipio notify my at all about purposely sending me the wrong color? Sigh...

If IQ Shield makes a screen protector for your device, it is the best screen protector for your device.

I have IQ Shield brand protectors on just about everything I own that has a screen and I absolutely recommend this protector for the Kindle Paperwhite. It is a wet application and includes a cloth and a small container of solution that is just water with a tiny amount of detergent to clean the screen and remove oil from your fingertips before you apply. You spray your fingers, you spray the protector, and you apply it to the device. The solution gives the protector just enough slide to be repositioned and adjusted to get the perfect fit. Once applied, you can use the provided squeegee to press out any air bubbles and smooth out the application. Give it a day or so to "cure" and you will hardly know it's there. I also appreciate how it doesn't completely go corner to corner in the recessed screen area. Just in case you ever have to remove it, you will be able to easily get a fingernail under the protector to remove without having to really dig at it and potentially damage your screen. The touch screen of the kindle functions perfectly and the flat matte plastic adds no glare which is appreciated as some screen protectors completely negate the low glare nature of e-ink displays. None of that here.

I emailed Caseology about this because they say to email them with any concerns and this is certainly a concern of mine. I would hate to crack the screen on my new $900 phone with a "Military Grade Drop Tested" case. No response so far but it's the weekend so I will give them some time to see what they say.

UPDATE 2022-10-27: Caseology had plenty of time to respond to my concerns... and nothing. They should not be asking people to email them with concerns if they are just going to ignore them or take a ridiculous time to respond. Ever since I dropped my first iPhone 5S, years ago and cracked the screen I have become an iPhone case fanatic and over the years have purchased a number of cases for the phones while looking for the perfect case. I have purchased slim, TPU cases which are light, but provide very little or no protection at all. I have also purchased protective rugged cases that have provided excellent protection, but are very thick and add a lot of bulk and weight to the phone and when placed in my pocket it seems like I'm carrying a brick.
Recently I purchased the X-Doria Defense Lux Carbon Fiber back case, which I've been using for months. It provides very good protection, and although it is not as bulky as the others, it does feel very heavy in my pocket. Which brings me to this X-Doria Defense Shield case. Like I said before, I've been looking for "my perfect case."
Why am I sticking with X-Doria? Because the cases provide very good Military Grade protection for the phone and they don't seem as bulky as other cases.
I tried the Otterbox Defender, but the wireless charging kept turning on and off and often times in the am the phone was almost dead.
Regarding this case, I was undecided whether to purchase the X-Doria Defense Air series or this Defense Shield, but based on some of the bad reviews from other shoppers for the Air I decided to go with the Defense Shield. So far, I like the case. It provides good protection and it is lighter than the Lux. The ONLY thing that I do not like about the case is that the back polycarbonate back scratches easily, in less than one day of minimal use it is already scratched. (The only reason why I did not give it 5 stars). However, the black color covers it up slightly.
In any case, if I have to choose between a case that will not protect the phone, a case that feels like a brick or a case that provides good protection and is lighter, but the back shows scratches; I'll take the slight scratch marks, and I'll keep using my new X-Doria Defense Shield case.
BTW> Just to be on the safe side, base on other reviews I've read, I purchased the TETHYS glass screen protector which I will be installing on the phone tomorrow when I receive it.

I had been really happy with my Moto X Pure for several months, but I wasn't crazy about how big it was - so I decided to downsize with the S7 after seeing all the glowing reviews.

The S7 is indeed the perfect size for a phone; my slightly smaller than average (for a guy) hands can reach everything with one had, and the glass body is beautiful to look at and to hold.

The features are great too - good battery life, fingerprint sensor, wireless charging, Samsung Pay (so you can pay via NFC or using conventional card readers), nice cameras, super-bright flashlight, even a pedometer!

The Samsung Touchwiz software is an unnecessary departure from standard Android but mostly it just looks a bit different and doesn't really do anything cool or new except for a bunch of semi-useless bells and whistles (although I really like that it lets you prevent certain notifications from appearing on the lock screen - my other Moto phone with standard Android can't do that).

The first two days I used the phone (no joke) it got 5 (that's five) system updates from Verizon - and it never worked right after that. The first sign of trouble was when it unexpectedly restarted in the middle of me using an app. Then every time after that, after rebooting, it would show the message "The process system isn't responding, do you want to close it?", and whether I chose "Wait" or "OK", the phone would be laggy, get warm, have trouble getting a signal, and fail to finish uninstalling any apps.

After a little online research, I decided to boot into recovery mode and clear the system cache - but that didn't help; neither did starting it in Safe Mode. Then I tried a couple of different apps to clear individual app's cache, but oddly they would just get stuck while scanning. Finally I tried the nuclear option, and did a factory reset. That seemed to do the trick, but once I had reinstalled all of my usual apps (that all work fine on my Moto X Pure), the "Process system isn't responding" error came back.

So I returned the phone. Maybe I just had a lemon, or maybe it was a bug in one of the software updates; I commend Samsung/Verizon for issuing updates frequently, but the more frequent updates are, the less time there is to test them, and I wonder if they've been taking shortcuts that let bugs slip through.

P.S. It's worth mentioning, though everyone already knows and complains about it, that Samsung and Verizon stuff these phones full of bloatware and redundant apps that you mostly can't uninstall. And some of the system updates added additional useless apps (mostly games).

UPDATE.....................

I did some research and it sounds like the problems I encountered could be caused by SD card incompatibility. Apparently the S7 (and I'm sure many other phones) are very picky about which SD cards work better or worse. Some users have reported the problems can be avoided by using the phone to re-format the SD card, and/or by not moving certain apps to the SD card.

It doesn’t have a raised edge around the screen (if you put the phone face down on a flat surface the screen will fully touch it), and the material is more slippery to hold than the naked phone, so I always felt like I was more likely to drop the phone.

Advantage of kickstand:
-No need to purchase/keep track of a separate stand as it's essentially built in to the case once installed

-Better grip while holding your phone. Good for when you're on a busy bus or train where your body sways back and forth during erupt stops or when other people bump into you when trying to get off the train/bus

Disadvantage:
-Kickstand provide low viewing angle. May be a problem if you're one who like stands that lifts the phone several inches off the flat surface

-Not able to lay your phone entirely flat, unless you have the screen facing down which loses the wave to wake functionality to look it the time or notifications
